evidence_quotes.quote: play video games with great pleasure.
‘live service’ games or other games with online functionalities.
What happens to the games if developers decide that they no longer want to operate the servers?
an ‘end of life’ plan so that such games remain playable even after the servers have been switched off.

paragraphs.text: Dear European Commission, I have been able to think with my heart a “game” and play video games with great pleasure. They offer a fun and relaxing alternative to working day or simply meeting and enjoying friends. However, there have been unwelcome developments in the gaming sector for some time. On the one hand, it is the ‘online forced’ of various games, even if they do not have any online elements at all. (But this reason is not at all the main trigger of this letter) Secondly, this letter deals with ‘live service’ games or other games with online functionalities. The problem is: What happens to the games if developers decide that they no longer want to operate the servers? They switch them off because it would not be economically viable for them to continue operating them. Your right! But at the same time, it is often the case that this also deprives me of the access and playability of my purchased game for which I spent money. In my view, however, it would be possible, in my view, to play a game which I have acquired on a permanent basis. There is a conflict of interest between the two groups. There are ways to keep games with online elements in play even after the servers have been switched off, whether through self-hosted community servers or simply ‘switching off’ the online component without making the game completely unplayable. Unfortunately, large game developers and lobby groups have the longer lever in this conflict of interest. You can simply switch off the servers and leave players on drying with your purchased games. After some time, the turmoil will be forgotten and you will simply continue. What remains to be left to us as EU citizens is our right to do so here. This requires legislation to encourage publishers to develop an ‘end of life’ plan so that such games remain playable even after the servers have been switched off. The Stop Killing Games Initiative (Stop Killing Games Initiative) has already collected over 1 million signatures in the EU. This describes my creation again better than what I wrote here. Here I see the possibility that the Digital Fairness Act could also cover this problem. How exactly do I leave to the EU’s competent lawmaker. I would like to thank you very much for your attention and ask for your support.
